paper_title: A Verifiable Search Is Not a Learnable Chain-of-Thought

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: It is tempting to assume any task solvable by a short program can be taught to a model as its chain-of-thought: write the steps out, fine-tune, and the model follows. This paper shows the assumption fails for an identifiable class of procedures. The testbed is nine reasoning tasks, each from a deterministic generator; public and hidden splits share generators, so held-out data proxies test accuracy. I reverse-engineer the generators into Python solvers, render them as chain-of-thought, and distill into a rank-<= 32 LoRA over a 30B (3.5B-active) Nemotron model. Forward-computable tasks install readily: lookup/arithmetic and an 8-bit boolean task transfer (>= 0.99 and 0.68). Cryptarithm does not: distilling its backtracking search holds at 0.01-0.07 across eleven chain-of-thought designs, RL from verifiable rewards, and self-training, even though a search solver answers 71% of instances. This is not a capability gap. The model does the arithmetic on 97-100% of lines and ranks the correct cipher in its top eight on 71%; it cannot carry the search forward as a left-to-right derivation. Fine-tuning learns the shape of a verifiable elimination step while its verdicts become unconditional templates, correct only 16-57% of the time ("verdict-as-token"). The ceiling holds across backbones from 3B to 671B and across fine-tuning and prompting; a controlled intervention isolates the cause: revealing the cipher key, which turns the derivation forward, lifts the same instances from 0.03 to 0.57. When a procedure's only solution is search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists to imitate. The task becomes learnable only by removing the search, precomputing its combinatorial core into a catalog and reducing the trace to recall plus verification; the 1st-place solution reaches Private LB 0.92 this way. What distills is memorization and verification, not search.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: It is tempting to assume that any task one can solve with short program can be taught to model as its chain-of-thought: write the programs steps out in words, fine-tune on them, and the model should follow. This paper shows the assumption fails for an identifiable class of procedures, and that the reflexive remedies do not rescue it. The testbed is nine reasoning tasks, each emitted by deterministic generator, with one convenient property: the public and hidden splits are drawn from the same generators, so held-out slice of training data is faithful proxy for test accuracy. reverse-engineer the generators into Python solvers (five reach 98%), render their procedures as chain-of-thought, and distill them into rank32 LoRA over 30B (3.5B-active) Nemotron model. Forward-computable tasks install readily: four lookup/arithmetic tasks and an 8-bit boolean-rule task transfer ( 0.99 and 0.68). Cryptarithm does not, when one tries to distill its backtracking search: it holds at 0.010.07 across eleven chain-of-thought designs, RL from verifiable rewards, and self-training, even though search solver answers 71% of its instances. The failure is not capability gap. The model does the arithmetic correctly on 97100% of lines and ranks the correct cipher into its top eight on 71% of instances. What it cannot do is carry the search forward as left-to-right derivation. Supervised fine-tuning therefore learns the shape of verifiable elimination step while its verdicts become unconditional templates, correct only 1657% of the time (verdict-as-token). This search-distillation ceiling holds across four backbones from 3B to 671B and across both fine-tuning and in-context prompting, and controlled intervention isolates the cause: revealing the cipher key, which turns the derivation forward, lifts the same instances from 0.03 to 0.57. The constraint binds the search, not the task: when procedures only solution is search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists to imitate. The task nonetheless becomes learnable by removing the search from the trace, precomputing its combinatorial core into finite catalog the model memorizes and reducing the chain-of-thought to recall plus bounded verification; the competitions 1st-place solution is the existence proof, reaching Private LB 0.92 by memorizing per-signature candidate catalog rather than teaching the model to search [8]. What distills is memorization and verification, not search. release the benchmark decomposition, the solvers, the per-line fidelity audits, and the control experiments. 6 2 0 2 0 2 ] . sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: If you can write short program that solves every instance of task, is the task learnable as chain-of-thought? Intuitively the program is recipe, the recipe can be written out as naturallanguage steps, and capable model fine-tuned on those steps should reproduce them. This intuition underlies large body of recent work on distilling reasoning into smaller models, from rationale 1 distillation [11] to bootstrapped self-training [31] and reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards [5, 16, 25]. This paper reports controlled study in which the intuition fails, sharply and reproducibly, for specific and identifiable class of procedures. use benchmark of nine reasoning tasks, each emitted by deterministic generator (Roman numerals, linear unit conversion, free-fall, monoalphabetic substitution cipher, an 8-bit boolean program, two equation-induction families, and two cryptarithm families). The benchmark has property that makes it an unusually clean laboratory: the hidden test set is produced by the same generators as the public training set, so the two are draws from one distribution and stratified held-out slice of training data predicts test accuracy directly. can therefore measure did the procedure transfer to fraction of point without access to the test labels. ask: (RQ1) does verifiable solvers coverage predict whether its Research questions. procedure is learnable as small models chain-of-thought? (RQ2) when procedure resists every training method try, what is the binding bottleneck? (RQ3) is there cheap, pre-training test that predicts resistance? In short: no, solver coverage does not predict whether the search distills (Figure 1); search over information-free structure admits no faithful forward CoT, so SFT learns only its unfaithful surface form (verdict-as-token), architecture-independently (Section 5.2). The binding bottleneck is forward-derivability, isolated by controlled intervention that lifts the same instances 0.03 0.57  (Table 7)  . The resistance is predictable in advance, via three-part screening heuristic (Section 7). And it is not ceiling on the task: the practical escape, demonstrated by the competitions 1st-place solution, is to memorize the searchs finite structure rather than distill the search itself (Section 9). first reverse-engineer the generators into Python solvers. Five of the nine tasks admit solvers at 98% accuracy. Cryptarithm admits backtracking-search solver that answers 71% of instances (the remainder are information-theoretically ambiguous). then attempt to install each solvers procedure into rank32 LoRA adapter [12] over Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B, hybrid Mamba-2/Mixture-of-Experts model [4, 22], served greedily under 7680-token budget. Training is SFT on solver-grounded synthetic CoT generated with different values from any evaluation instance (zero leakage); when SFT plateaus escalate to RLVR/GRPO and STaR. Thesis. verifiable search does not distill into chain-of-thought. The dividing line is whether the task admits faithful forward chain-of-thought, left-to-right derivation that reaches the answer. Forward-computable tasks install readily; task whose only solution is backtracking search over information-free structure has no faithful forward trace, so SFT copies the shape of the search but not its logic, across every architecture and scale tried. The task is not thereby unlearnable: it becomes learnable by removing the search from the trace, precomputing its combinatorial core into finite catalog the model memorizes and reducing the chain-of-thought to recall plus bounded verification (Section 9). What distills is memorization and verification, not search. Contributions. controlled solvabilitylearnability gap. On traintest benchmark quantify, per task, the gap between verifiable solvers accuracy and the accuracy the fine-tuned model actually reproduces in CoT (Figure 1, Table 1). The gap is near-zero for four tasks and exceeds 0.65 for cryptarithm. negative result with convergent evidence. Across eleven CoT-engineering rounds and three RL/self-training escalations, cryptarithm-deduce never exceeds 0.07 (Figure 4). Five 2 independent measurements, solver ceiling, information floor, base-model behavior, forwardderivation coverage, and per-line execution fidelity, each independently bound the achievable accuracy, and they agree  (Table 3)  . Three named, measured mechanisms. Verdict-as-token (Figure 5): arithmetic is correct on 97100% of lines but elimination verdicts are emitted as unconditional templates (fidelity 16 57%). Derivationvs. ranking-blocked: budget-unbounded generate-and-verify reframe shows ranking is not the constraint (hit@8 = 0.71) while forward derivation is (1/659). Information-free sub-structure: the hidden maps MI(digit; glyph) = 0.021 nats 0. positive control that confirms the mechanism. Hand-written bit-manipulation CoT that teleports the rule search collapses to 0.05; STaR on the models own successful searches lifts 0.526 0.678 and removes truncation (18.6% 0.2%, Figure 3). What transfers is search the model can actually run, not search narrated for it. An architecture control. Training the identical cryptarithm corpus on four backbones, hybrid Mamba/MoE, two dense Transformers, and an MoE Transformer, spanning 3B21B, hits the same 0.04 floor  (Table 6)  . The search-distillation ceiling is architecture-general, not backboneor scale-specific. This refutes my own initial SSM-specific hypothesis. causal intervention. Revealing the cipher key on the same cryptarithm instances, turning the derivation forward, lifts Nemotron from 0.03 to 0.571  (Table 7)  . Revealing only half barely helps. Forward-derivability is the causal lever, and faithful forward CoT must cover the whole derivation, not most of it. The escape, externally validated. The search-distillation ceiling is not ceiling on the task. The competitions 1st-place solution reaches Private LB 0.92 by memorizing the searchs finite structure, 4,205-entry per-signature candidate catalog for cryptarithm and 5,238-entry rulesequence catalog for bit-manipulation, and verifying candidates in-trace, rather than distilling the search [8]. This both confirms the mechanism, why memorization is necessary, and bounds the claim (Section 9). did not win the competition this benchmark is drawn from, my best adapter scored 0.85 public (0.86 private, on an unselected submission, Section 8) against leader at 0.92. As discuss in Section 8, 0.85 is the median of the 4,355-team field and the ceiling reachable without the memorizationcomputation reformulation of cryptarithm and bit-manipulation (Section 9); fully open-source solution taking my same search-distillation approach won the competitions Open Progress Prize at the same 0.85. The contribution is not ranking; it is clean, instrumented account of where and why verifiable search fails to become model reasoning, and of what does transfer in its place.

sections.title: 2 Background and Related Work
sections.content: Chain-of-thought and its distillation. Eliciting step-by-step reasoning improves LLM accuracy on multi-step tasks [29, 30], and models rationales can be distilled into smaller students [10, 11, 21]. My setting is distillation in the strong sense: the teacher is perfect symbolic solver, and ask whether its procedure survives transfer into small models CoT under budget. Self-training and verifiable rewards. STaR bootstraps model on its own correct rationales [31]; RL from verifiable rewards and group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) optimize directly against an answer checker [5, 16, 25], and process verifiers supervise intermediate steps [18]. The 3 generate-and-verify idea traces to training answer verifiers on grade-school math [3]. use all three as escalations. central finding is that on my hard task RLVRs gradient is present but non-transferring: it improves easy curricula yet does not move held-out accuracy, because positive rollouts on real instances are too rare to learn from. Faithfulness of chain-of-thought. growing literature shows that models stated reasoning need not reflect the computation behind its answer: explanations can be systematically unfaithful [28], and interventions on the trace (inserting mistakes, truncating, paraphrasing) often leave the answer unchanged [17], which motivates constructions that force the answer to follow from the stated steps [19]. My verdict-as-token result is sharp, mechanistic instance of unfaithfulness arising in the training regime: SFT installs the surface form of verifiable elimination step while decoupling it from the steps logic, so the imitated trace is unfaithful by construction at free-running inference. Limits of transformers on procedures. My negative result is consistent with, and sharpens, work on compositional and algorithmic limits: transformers degrade on multi-step composition as depth grows [7] and struggle to generalize algorithm length [1]. Length generalization holds only for tasks expressible as short length-invariant programs [32], memory-free architectures cannot climb the Chomsky hierarchy [6], algorithmic competence can emerge only after delayed generalization [23], and models score poorly even when worked step-by-step solutions exist [9]. add mechanism specific to imitation learning of search, verdict-as-token under teacher forcing, and connect it to the classic exposure-bias gap between teacher-forced training and free-running inference [2, 24]. Architecture. The base model is hybrid in which most attention layers are replaced by Mamba-2 state-space layers, interleaved with sparse Mixture-of-Experts [4, 22], and served with pagedattention batching [15]. State-space layers compress history into fixed-size state; will argue this interacts unfavorably with tasks that require explicit, unbounded search-state. Only 6 of the models 52 layers carry attention; the bulk are Mamba-2 or MoE. The rank32 adapter train spans the attention, Mamba, MoE, and output projections alike (Section C), but rank-32 update is thin slice of model whose capacity sits overwhelmingly in layers that summarize history into fixed state. There is theory for why this should bite: state-space layers lie in TC0 and cannot perform genuine sequential state-tracking [20], and fixed-size recurrent state caps copying and retrieval from context [13]. tested this directly, however, and find it is not the explanation for cryptarithm  (Table 6)  : dense and MoE-Transformer models of comparable active size hit the same floor, so the SSM limit is at most contributing factor (e.g. for the bit-manipulation tail), not the cause of the search-distillation ceiling.

sections.title: 3 The Testbed: a Deterministic-Generator Benchmark
sections.content: Task and grading. The benchmark ships public training set of 9,500 labeled problems and is scored on hidden test set of 500 problems. submission is single LoRA adapter of rank at most 32 loaded onto the frozen base model and served with vLLM in deterministic (greedy) mode (temperature 0, top-p 1.0, max_tokens= 7680, max_model_len= 8192), with thinking enabled (<think>...</think>). The model must place its final answer in boxed{} field; the grader extracts the last such field (falling back to the last number) and applies fixed verify(): exact case-insensitive string match for binary strings, relative tolerance 102 for floats, exact string match otherwise. 4 The traintest property. Every category is produced by deterministic generator, and the hidden test uses the same generators. Train and test are therefore i.i.d. draws from one distribution, and stratified held-out slice of train.csv (100 rows/category) is faithful oracle for the leaderboard. exploit this throughout: train on synthetic CoT built with different values (zero leakage) and evaluate on real held-out training rows, treating that number as the test accuracy. determinism check confirms the regime: when an in-class rule fits problems worked examples, it yields unique prediction on the query 98.7% of the time and is correct 99.8% of the time, the examples pin the rule, so the only limits to 100% are solver and model completeness, not intrinsic ambiguity (cryptarithm, below, is the exception that proves this). The nine generators. Table 1 lists the categories, their test weights, and the base models zero-shot accuracy. Briefly: numeral (write an integer in Roman numerals), unit_conversion (a linear map = ax + pinned by two examples), gravity (free-fall = 1 2 gt2, one-parameter fit), and cipher (a monoalphabetic substitution over fixed 77-word vocabulary, solved by aligning example word-pairs and constraint-propagating the rest) are the easy four. bit_manipulation maps 8 bits to 8 bits by single global boolean function over up to three taps (rotated/shifted/negated copies of the input), the same function for all eight output bits, pinned by 710 examples. equation_numeric (deduce/guess) shows visible-digit equations AB [op] CD = RHS from which one must induce the operand pairing, the operation, and an output-string format. cryptarithm (deduce/guess) is the hard case, described next. Cryptarithm structure. Each example is five-character left-hand side s0 s1 OP s2 s3 = RHS. Three things are hidden: (1) per-row injective digitsymbol cipher (a uniform draw of 10 distinct glyphs from 23-symbol pool); (2) an operation attached to the middle operator glyph, drawn from wide vocabulary (add, sub, mul, abs-difference, mod, gcd, lcm, floor-div, the 1/ 2 variants, and two purely structural concat operations). And (3) per-row endianness (standard or little-endian, reversing operand and result strings). Deduce means the querys operator was seen in the examples; guess means it was not. Because the cipher is information-free, no single column reveals digit: the map is pinned only by requiring all example equations to hold simultaneously under the right operations and direction, cross-equation constraint search with backtracking. This is the structural reason cryptarithm sits on the far side of the learnability frontier. Compute and budget. Final adapters are trained on single RTX PRO 6000 (Blackwell, 96 GB) via the competition platform. The adapter has 880M trainable parameters (2.71% of the model). Cheap iteration on data and CoT design is done on managed LoRA service at $3.5/round before promoting finalized dataset to the full training run, three-tier cheapest-first ladder (API probe cheap LoRA full train) that found essential for keeping long negative-result search affordable. All CoT is written in plain ASCII: the tokenizer is 131,072-entry BPE with no <unk>, but Unicode operators (, , =) fragment into rare multi-byte tokens, so spell operators as words (XOR, NOT), arrows as ->, and inequality as !=.

For cryptarithm the solver reaches 0.71, but the forward-derivable ceiling collapses to 0.10 and the model tracks that, not the solver (0.05). bit_manipulation is the exception where forward derivation is high yet the model still gaps, search-depth limit STaR partly closes (Section 5.1). The guess tasks are information-limited. Exact values and CIs in Table 1. 6 by instrumenting the solver to narrate its own steps in the base models native ASCII style, ending in boxed{}; (3) SFT the LoRA on this synthetic corpus. And (4) evaluate the adapter on held-out real training rows. Because step (2) uses different values than any evaluation row, there is no leakage; because of the traintest property, step (4) is the leaderboard. deliberately enforce one CoT discipline throughout: the trace must show the search (try reject match) and must never teleport to an answer it has not derived on the page. trace that states the conclusion without the work teaches nothing transferable; this principle, and its violations, turn out to be the crux of the whole study (Section 7). render CoT by instrumenting the solver to Rendering, and what witnessed means. narrate each step in the bases native ASCII voice: transcribe the relevant glyphs, compute, and emit verdict (keep, drop, this run extends). The one non-negotiable rule is that every verdict must restate, on its own line, the evidence that forces it, witnessed verdict. verdict that is fixed phrase decoupled from the lines own numbers is teleport: it reads as reasoning but carries none. Figure 2 contrasts the two on real ones-digit elimination; the failure shown is verbatim from trained model and is the modal cryptarithm error (Figure 5). Witnessed verdict (rendered) vs. verdict-as-token (emitted) WITNESSED: EQ1 ones: 6. TELEPORT: EQ1 ones: ends 6 -> none matches -> drop. ... target ones = 4 (= ones). try ones 6: 6*4 ends 4 -> MATCH -> keep 3*4 ends 2; 5*4 ends 0; 6*4 ends 4; 7*4 ends 8; 8*4 ends 2; 9* Figure 2: Witnessed vs. teleported verdict. The teleport line is verbatim from trained models transcript (10b71e8a): it computes 6*4 ends 4, which matches the target ones digit 4, on the very same line, then concludes none matches drop, wrongly eliminating the correct operation. The arithmetic is correct; the verdict does not follow from it. SFT on traces whose verdicts are not witnessed installs exactly this behavior. When SFT plateaus on category escalate along two axes: RLVR/GRPO against the binary verify() reward, and STaR, harvesting the models own correct rollouts (filtered by the verifier) and folding them back as SFT data. The escalations cost more, so gate them: round is only promoted if cheap held-out probe clears pre-registered threshold. Statistical reporting. Accuracies are point estimates reported with Wilson 95% confidence intervals and the evaluation size n. Held-out sets are real-train slices (Section 3): 100/category unless noted, with bit-manipulation evaluated on disjoint 500-row set and cryptarithm on the n=93/87 unseen deduce/guess slices. flag explicitly where small makes difference non-significant (it does for the pass@k comparisons. It does not for the headline solvermodel gaps).

sections.title: 5 Results
sections.content: My version-by-version leaderboard trajectory (Section F) situates the project. The frozen base model scores 0.466; null (zero) adapter reproduces it (0.50). Installing the four easy tasks and first bit-manipulation CoT reaches 0.59. from-base adapter with finalized cipher/bit/equation grammars banks 0.85. On the final 4,355-team leaderboard 0.85 is the median: 2,236 teams reached 7 0.85, only 66 cleared 0.87, and just the leader reached 0.92 (Section 8). My 0.85 is the solver ceiling of the tasks could make learnable; the missing 0.07 to the top is almost entirely cryptarithm and the bit-manipulation tail. The rest of this section dissects those two.

sections.title: 5.1 Where the procedure transfers: the easy four and bit_manipulation
sections.content: The four lookup/fit tasks are uninteresting in the best way: once the CoT is correct and ASCII-clean, the model reproduces the solver to 0.99. They establish that the pipeline, the budget, and the grader are not the bottleneck. Bit-manipulation is the informative middle of the frontier, and the place where knowing the generator pays off most. The tasks own prompt names its operations: The transformation involves operations like bit shifts, rotations, XOR, AND, OR, NOT, and possibly majority or choice functions. This is gift, it says the hidden rule is single boolean function over up to three taps (rotated/shifted copies of the 8-bit input), drawn from small human-nameable vocabulary and applied identically to all eight output bits. make that explicit: six-function basis {xor, maj, xor_or, or, or_xnor, choice} (two 2-argument and four 3-argument functions; Table 2) accounts, under first-match partition, for 1577 of the 1602 training rows. The remaining 25 need deeper composition. and-of-three is redundant, its rows are subsumed by majority (108/109). This is the same single-rule space my global solver searches by op-composition to 98.9% query accuracy (Section B). The basis is its interpretable face, mapping one-to-one onto the generators stated vocabulary. The point is the contrast. The solver side of bit-manipulation is essentially closed: handful of named functions over handful of taps explains the entire dataset. Against that, the fine-tuned models 0.678 is not data problem, it is learnability problem, and sharply structured one  (Table 4)  : the rules the model misses are overwhelmingly the three-tap ones (34% of the category, accuracy 0.50), exactly the rules whose search is deepest. The procedure, enumerate the basis tap-assignments, verify on all examples, apply, is the bounded search expected to be teachable. It is, but only in one specific way. Table 2: six-function basis for the bit-manipulation rule, mapping onto the generators own prompt vocabulary. Each function takes up to three taps (independent rotated/shifted copies of the input) and is applied identically to all eight output bits. Counts are priority-ordered first-match partition of the 1602 training rows. The op-composition solver attains 98.9% query accuracy on the same rows (Section B). Figure 3: Bit-manipulation: only the models own search transfers. Hand-written CoT that teleports the rule search scores like the base model (0.053). STaR on verifier-passed self-traces lifts accuracy and collapses budget truncation, because the imitated traces are searches the model can actually run within budget. BaselineSTaR is significant (disjoint 95% CIs, n=500). The r1r2 step is within noise. grammar that named the taps then asserted the rule (0.062), and peel-to-two-tap scheme (0.053). The common defect, confirmed by line-level autopsy of 28 wrong output bits (Section 7), is that each authored trace teleports the one step that matters, the rule search, because human author already knows the answer and cannot un-know it while writing. Transcription, arithmetic, and assembly in these traces are flawless. Only the search verdict is fake. What worked was STaR. Seeding from weak warm start, harvested the models own verifierpassed rollouts and fine-tuned on them. Accuracy rose 0.526 0.656 0.678 over two rounds, and, tellingly, budget truncation fell from 18.6% to 0.2% (Figure 3). The models own successful searches are short, decisive, and (because they actually ran) honest. Imitating them installs procedure the model can execute at inference, closing the exposure-bias gap that the hand-written traces opened. Accuracy then plateaus: the residual 0.50 on genuine three-tap rules is the bases search ceiling on this architecture, and additional synthetic three-tap data did not beat the STaR number (0.602 < 0.678).

sections.title: 5.2 Where it does not: cryptarithm
sections.content: Cryptarithm is the negative result, and it is robust. ran eleven successive CoT designs (r1r11; Figure 4 plots the nine that reached held-out greedy eval, r4 having been gate-failed before training): decode-first verification, truthful conditionals, policy-driven backtracking renderers, two-regime structure modeling, construction-of-symbol-table scaffolds, monotone try-counters with bail-out endings, self-routing forced chains, witness-bearing elimination lines, generate-and-verify reframe, and explicit terminate-on-exhaustion traces. Every round lands in [0.01, 0.07]. The truncation rate swings wildly across rounds, from 87% down to 1%, while accuracy does not move, which already rules out ran out of budget as the explanation. then measured the ceiling five independent ways  (Table 3)  . (1) An Five walls converge. unbounded full-enumeration vote, the solver with no token limit, answers 457/659 = 0.693; 25.8% of instances are genuinely ambiguous given the examples. (2) The hidden map is information-free: MI(digit; glyph) = 0.021 nats versus shuffle baseline of 0.022, and battery of structural predictors (ascii-offset, pool-linear, first-appearance order, frequency argmax) all score at or below the analytic chance rate of 0.069 on leave-one-digit-out prediction; the two gap strata that would require 9 r a d _ i p 0. 0.4 0.2 0 search-solver 0.71 0.03 0.07 r2 0.05 r3 0.04 0.05 r6 0.07 r7 0.05 forward-derivable 0.10 0.01 r9 0.01 r10 Figure 4: One floor, many rounds. The nine reaching held-out greedy eval are shown; RLVR/GRPO and the generate-andverify reframe ran between rounds; Table 12 lists all fourteen training runs). The cleanest single diagnostic is generate-andverify reframe: instead of deriving the map, the model proposes hypotheses and learned prior ranks them. Here hit@8 = 0.71 (the gold answer is in the models top-8 on 71% of instances) and the worst-case token cost of checking eight hypotheses is well under budget, so ranking is not the constraint. When instead require the model to derive each hypothesis by forward arithmetic closure, coverage on the top-8 collapses to 1/659 and the projected yield (0.205) falls below my render gate (0.45), so the round was never trained. The model can recognize correct map but cannot construct one left-to-right, because constructing it requires tracking which assignments are still consistent, explicit search state, across the whole problem. Verdict-as-token. The mechanism becomes concrete in per-line fidelity audit of 100 held-out transcripts (7,566 line records), scored on three axes: transcription, arithmetic, and verdict (does lines stated conclusion follow from its own numbers?). Arithmetic is essentially perfect, the dominant elimination line type (enumerate ones-residues, none matches, drop this candidate; = 390) has 100% arithmetic correctness, yet its verdict is correct only 34.9% of the time: the model drops candidates that, by its own numbers on the same line, actually match. Other elimination types show the same split (A=100%, =16.5%; A=97%, =51%). The median trace derails at its 552nd token. The model has learned the form of verifiable elimination step as fixed template, decoupled from the logic the step is supposed to encode. Under teacher forcing this template is always emitted in context where it happened to be correct. At free-running inference it fires unconditionally, textbook exposure-bias failure [2, 24], but localized to the single most load-bearing token in the procedure.

sections.title: 5.3 The solvability–learnability gap, quantified
sections.content: Table 1 and Figure 1 make the headline claim precise. The correlation between solver accuracy and model accuracy is high among the lookup/fit tasks and breaks exactly where the procedure stops being forward computation and becomes search: bit-manipulation (solver 0.99, model 0.68) and 11 cryptarithm-deduce (search-solver 0.71, model 0.05). The information-limited guess tasks are the control: there the solver itself is low (the answer is not determined by the prompt), and the model tracks it closely (0.18 vs 0.21; 0.02 vs 0.59 where the guess is bottlenecked by the upstream cryptarithm search). Solver coverage, in short, predicts model accuracy only when the solver is forward map. For search procedures it predicts almost nothing, unless the search is removed from the trace by memorizing its structure (Section 9).

sections.title: 6 Anatomy of the Failures
sections.content: I characterize the failures at the line and token level across all nine tasks (Table 5. Full per-subcategory numbers in Section E). Two facts frame everything. First, for the shipped adapters, failure is wrong-answer, not budget: cryptarithm-deduce finishes within budget and is wrong on 96% of instances (1% truncated), and bit-manipulation truncates on 0% (17% finished-but-wrong). The levers that matter are correctness levers, not length levers. The one exception is diagnostic in itself: the rendered cryptarithm search traces (backtracking narrated step by step) loop and truncate on 6472% of instances, because the procedure needs tried-set the model does not maintain. Second, the errors are not arithmetic: 28-output-bit autopsy and 7,566-line cryptarithm fidelity audit both find transcription and arithmetic essentially correct, what fails is the decision: which rule, which verdict. Bit-manipulation: the failures are rule-selection on hard taps. Accuracy is clean function of tap count  (Table 4)  : oneand two-tap rules are largely solved, three-tap rules sit at chancecorrected 0.50. An autopsy of 28 wrong output bits across 12 transcripts found zero transcription or arithmetic errors; 21 were fixable rule-selection slips (a dropped shift-anchor that shifts run by one, run accept/reject steps whose lines did not restate the evidence that should have decided them, and tie-breaks), and 7 were genuine three-tap bits for which no 2-input rule exists. Bit accuracy is therefore bounded by 0.82 in any per-bit grammar and by 0.50 on three-tap rows in any grammar the base can actually execute, which is exactly where STaR plateaus. Table 4: Bit-manipulation accuracy by tap count (best adapter, disjoint 500-row eval). Difficulty is monotone in the number of input taps the rule combines; the three-tap stratum is the residual ceiling. Rule complexity 1 tap 2 taps 3 taps overall Accuracy (95% CI) 0.881 [.75, .95] 42 0.763 [.71, .81] 283 0.503 [.43, .58] 169 500 0.678 [.64, .72] Share small 56% 34% , Cryptarithm: the verdict is decoupled from the evidence. The fidelity audit (Figure 5) localizes the failure to elimination verdicts. The signature is concrete and reproducible: the model enumerates candidate residue pairs, writes one that matches the target on the same line, and then concludes none matches drop. The first false line arrives at median of token 552. When instead render the search explicitly so the model must run it, it either loops forever (69% of the generate-and-verify round re-enter an identical attempt, having no record of what was tried) or, 12 when forced to terminate, teleports to guess (31%, correct 1/31). Both are the absence of carried search state, seen from two sides. The native-voice attractor (a starkest-case exposure bias). distinct and, think, broadly important mode surfaced when authored grammars in non-native voice (traces opening with Bit.../Group... rather than the bases habitual I...). Teacher-forced fidelity on these grammars was 0.920.95, the model demonstrably learned them, yet at greedy decoding it entered them 0% of the time: at the very first generated token it emits its native opener instead, 1516 nat divergence, and never reaches the learned procedure. The grammar is fully-learned but unreachable island. Cryptarithm, whose traces happen to share the native opener, does enter the grammar and then drifts mid-trace, which is how it manages to score below constant-output baseline (destructive interference with the bases own attempt). The operational lesson is sharp: author chain-of-thought in the bases native voice, or it will never be decoded at inference no matter how well it is fit. This is exposure bias [2, 24] at its most extreme, the training and free-running distributions diverge at token 0. sections.title: 7 Analysis: Why Some Procedures Resist Distillation
sections.content: My results compose into one account, in three threads. Thread 1 is controlled refutation: distilling the search fails on every backbone, not just the hybrid one (every backbone caps). Thread 2 identifies the failure mechanism: SFT installs the surface form of verifiable step, verdict decoupled from its evidence. Thread 3 gives the root reason: the search admits no faithful forward chain-of-thought to imitate. The chain is: no faithful forward trace SFT copies only the shape verdict-as-token collapse at free-running inference, on every backbone. The dual escape, memorizing the searchs finite structure rather than distilling the search, is taken up in Section 9. 1. The bottleneck is the task, not the architecture. The base model has every primitive cryptarithm needs, it does column arithmetic correctly 97100% of the time, recognizes correct map 71% of the time when one is proposed, and handles the purely transcriptional concat family. What it cannot do is run search: maintain the set of tried assignments, propagate constraints, and decide branch is dead. first suspected the hybrid Mamba-2/MoE backbone: state-space 13 layers compress history into fixed-size state [4] that sits in TC0 and cannot perform genuine sequential state-tracking [20], natural reason growing search frontier cannot be carried. controlled architecture sweep refutes that explanation for this task  (Table 6)  : trained on the identical cryptarithm corpus, two dense Transformers (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3.5-4B) and an MoE Transformer (gpt-oss-20b) hit the same 0.04 floor, each fits the corpus (train NLL floor) yet reproduces almost none of it at inference. The search-distillation failure is therefore architecturegeneral, and the binding constraint is task-intrinsic, no faithful forward CoT to imitate (thread 3), not the SSM backbone. (State compression may still contribute to the bit-manipulation three-tap tail, where rank32 adapter is too small perturbation to install search memory.) The same holds without fine-tuning: sampled in-context under the competitions 7680-token budget, DeepSeek-V3.1 (671B) scores 0.05 (finishes, wrong) and Nemotron-Super-120B scores 0.00 (truncates on 100%), so it spans 3B671B and SFTin-context alike  (Table 6)  . The bit autopsy is the same story in miniature: of 28 wrong output bits, 0 were transcription or arithmetic errors. All traced to run-extension verdicts (accept/reject/tie-break) whose lines did not restate the evidence that should have decided them. Table 6: The search does not distill, on any backbone. Cryptarithm-deduce accuracy, all under the competitions 7680-token budget. Top: the identical corpus fine-tuned (rank-32 LoRA, 4 epochs) on four backbones, 3B30B. Bottom: two frontier models sampled in-context (no fine-tuning. The prompt already carries the worked examples). Spanning 3B671B and fine-tunedin-context, none reproduces the search. At frontier scale the failure splits: V3.1 finishes but is wrong. The 120B truncates on 100% within budget. The exact 30B/A3B Transformer match (Qwen3-30B-A3B) was unschedulable on my compute. Model Params (tot/act) Architecture deduce note Fine-tuned (rank-32 LoRA, identical corpus; n=100) 30B / 3.5B 3B / 3B 4B / 4B 21B / 3.6B Nemotron-3-Nano Llama-3.2-3B Qwen3.5-4B gpt-oss-20b Hybrid Mamba-2+MoE 0.030.05 Dense Transformer Dense Transformer MoE Transformer 0.010 0.040 0.010 In-context, no fine-tuning (raw base; n=20) DeepSeek-V3.1 Nemotron-Super 671B / 37B 120B / 12B MoE Transformer Hybrid Mamba+MoE 0.050 0. finishes, wrong 100% truncate 2. Verdict-as-token is the failures signature under teacher forcing. verifiable solvers most important lines are its verdicts, this candidate is impossible, this run extends. When CoT is written so that verdict is fixed phrase rather than function of locally-written evidence, SFT learns the phrase, not the function, because under teacher forcing the phrase is always seen in correct context. The defect is invisible in training loss, across fourteen cryptarithm training rounds the train NLL converged to the floor (e.g. crypt-r1 reaches NLL 0.146 by step 35 and 0.001 by the end; the Kaggle merge runs log final loss 0.003) while held-out deduce accuracy never left [0.01, 0.07]  (Table 12)  , and invisible under teacher-forced evaluation. It appears only at free-running greedy decode, as the verdict firing in the wrong context. This is why every authored trace failed and only self-harvested traces (STaR) succeeded for bit-manipulation: self-traces verdict is, by construction, one the model produced and the verifier confirmed, so the evidenceverdict link is real rather than narrated. 14 3. The root cause: no faithful forward CoT to imitate. Cryptarithm is the extreme case: the hidden map is information-free (wall 2), so there is no left-to-right derivation that human or solver could write down that arrives at the map by forward reasoning, the only honest route is backtracking search, which forward CoT cannot represent without carried state. The forward-closure coverage of 1/659 is the formal statement: the set of instances solvable by the steps the base can actually execute, in order, is empty for all practical purposes. When no faithful forward CoT exists, SFT can only learn an unfaithful one, and the unfaithful one is exactly the verdict-as-token template that collapses at inference. predictive rule of thumb. Together these suggest cheap pre-test for whether solvable task will be CoT-learnable on small model, applied before any training: (a) does purely forward closure (no backtracking, no global constraint propagation) cover meaningful fraction of instances? (b) does the tasks hidden structure carry non-trivial mutual information with observable tokens? (c) does the base model, prompted natively, attempt the right kind of move rather than truncating? Cryptarithm fails all three; bit-manipulation passes (a) and (c) and is learnable via the models own search; the easy four pass trivially. derived this post-hoc from the nine tasks here, but test its core claim, that forward-derivability is the causal lever, prospectively next  (Table 7)  ; the broader three-part heuristic remains hypothesis offer for testing. prospective test: the forward-derivability intervention. To check the core claim causally rather than by correlation, took the same cryptarithm instances and revealed fraction of the digitsymbol key in the prompt, turning that fraction of the derivation from search into forward lookup, then fine-tuned Nemotron on each (Table 7; n=42). With no key (the original task) accuracy is 0.03; revealing half the key barely moves it (0.048). Revealing the full key lifts it to 0.571, 15 jump on an otherwise identical task (disjoint 95% CIs), with the residual gap to 1.0 explained by ordinary op-execution errors (cf. equation_numeric at 0.83). Two things follow. First, forward-derivability is causal: it is the lever, holding the surface task fixed. Second, the effect is sharply nonlinear, half-forward does not give half-accuracy, because any residual search re-introduces the verdict-as-token failure; faithful forward CoT must cover the whole derivation, not most of it. Table 7: Forward-derivability is causal. The same cryptarithm instances, with fraction of the cipher key revealed in the prompt (turning that fraction of the derivation forward), fine-tuned on Nemotron (n=42, Wilson 95% CI). Making the task fully forward lifts accuracy 15. Half-forward barely helps, any residual search re-triggers the collapse. Key revealed Forward-derivable crypt-deduce none (original task) half full 0 50% 100% 0.03 0.048 [.01, .16] 0.571 [.42, .71]

sections.title: 8.1 Leaderboard structure and external corroboration
sections.content: The benchmark is drawn from public competition [14], whose final 4,355-team leaderboard is itself evidence for my thesis. The score distribution has sharp plateau exactly where place the frontier: 2,236 teams reached 0.85, only 66 cleared 0.87, 7 cleared 0.88, and just two teams reached 0.90, 15 with single leader at 0.92. score of 0.85 is the median. This is the shape my analysis predicts: the easy four plus learnable share of bit-manipulation get competent team to 0.85, and the last few points require cracking cryptarithm, which almost no one did. Independent corroboration of the ceiling. The competitions Open Progress Prize (awarded for the best reproducible methodology, separately from the raw leaderboard) went to fully opensource solution [26, 27] that, independently of me, took the same approach, reverse-engineer each generator, then SFT rank-32 LoRA on solver-grounded synthetic chain-of-thought (LoRA rank 32, adapt MLP/attention/unembed, LR 2 104, single epoch, 8192 context, category-stratified batching). It also scored 0.85. Two independent teams converging on the same method and the same ceiling is strong evidence that 0.85 is the ceiling of the search-distillation approach both used, not of either teams execution; the 1st-place solutions memorizationcomputation reformulation breaks past it to 0.92 (Section 9). My training recipe and theirs match closely (Section C), which is no coincidence: ours was tuned toward the same proven regime. The residual is generator hardness, not modeling laziness. My own frontier baseline agrees: DeepSeek-V3.1 (671B) and Nemotron-Super-120B, prompted in-context on cryptarithm, score 0.05 and 0.00  (Table 6)  . And third-party effort reports several hundred of the hardest puzzles unsolved even by frontier teacher models given the recovered rule at high reasoning effort. The ceiling is in distilling the search, not in failure to try.

sections.title: 8.2 Threats to validity
sections.content: Three are worth recording. (1) The metric has binary-string trap: binary answers are compared as exact strings, so correct value at the wrong width ("11011" vs "00011011") is scored wrong; this is hazard to avoid (emit the exact width), not lever. (2) Public/private overfit: competitors discussed choosing cryptarithm default operation (reversevs. forward-concatenation) that raised the public score while lowering the private one, leaderboard-specific artifact, and caution for anyone reading single-split numbers. (3) Memorization under traintest: with train and test from one generator, multi-epoch training can drive the training metric to ceiling by memorizing rather than learning the procedure, which is precisely why train on synthetic instances with different values and report held-out (unseen) accuracy rather than the contaminated headline (Section E). concrete leaderboard-gaming technique can document rather than merely report is adapter-noise laundering: notebook in my own repository loads third partys shared submission adapter and adds Gaussian noise at 20% of each tensors standard deviation to all 12,010 weight tensors, then repackages it as fresh submission. The perturbation is small enough to preserve most of the donors accuracy while yielding nominally distinct artifact. did not measure its leaderboard effect (my copy records no score), so report the procedure as reproducible integrity concern, not result, shared high-scoring adapter can be re-skinned into many ostensibly independent submissions. My own public/private near-miss (verified). The selection risk was not hypothetical for me. My full submission record (Kaggle API; Table 8) shows my best private score was 0.860, nativeHuggingFace run (EXP-138, forking Tongs published recipe [27]) trained on the 0.86-adapters verifier-passed traces plus 1,600 synthetic bit puzzles, yet that submission scored only 0.844 public, while my public-best submissions sat at 0.8520.856. The trap is sharpest in the reference adapter benchmarked: 0.856 public but 0.832 private (it overfit the public split). Standard public-LB selection would have discarded my 0.860-private model and kept weaker one. The public score, 16 computed on few-hundred-row split, is lossy selector. genuinely stronger model can go unselected, which is how medal-class private result was left on the table. Table 8: Public vs. private leaderboard for my key submissions (Kaggle API record). The ordering inverts: my best private score (0.860, EXP-138) is mid-pack on public, while the best public score (the reference adapter, 0.856) is among the worst on private. Selecting on the public column picks the wrong submission. sections.title: 9 Memorization, not search
sections.content: The search-distillation ceiling documented above is ceiling on distilling the search, not on the task. The competitions 1st-place solution (team NullSira; Private LB 0.920) reaches high cryptarithm and bit-manipulation accuracy by an approach its authors describe as deciding what the model should memorize . . . and what it should compute inside the trace [8], and it is the cleanest illustration of the present thesis: it succeeds precisely by not teaching the model to search. The naive search over cryptarithm prompt is intractable to narrate, up to 10! digit assignments times 243 operator rules ( 5 1010 candidates), which cannot be enumerated within the 7680token budget. The winning solution removes that search from the trace. For single equation it precomputes signature, the pattern of repeated symbols normalized by first occurrence (e.g. ABCCCDD), and enumerates all two-digit two-digit operands under the 22 non-join rules into catalog of 4,205 signatures, each mapping to short list of candidate (rule, digit-assignment) rows with counts. The model memorizes this catalog through heavy SFT; at inference it recalls the candidate list for each equation rather than deriving it. The trace then does only the cheap residual: pick the equation with the fewest candidates as an anchor, and run bounded DFS that checks the recalled candidates against the remaining equations (same symbol same digit, same operator same rule), pruning by mode and group constraints. The combinatorially hard step, candidate generation for the first equation, is memorized; only short, bounded verification remains as chain-of-thought. Bit-manipulation is handled the same way: 5,238 valid 8-rule sequences (every coherent 8-bit transformation, normalized to per-output-bit rules) are precomputed and memorized, the greedy perbit search is projected onto the nearest catalog sequence under Hamming distance, and candidates are verified against the examples (bit columns HEX-compressed to fit the budget). In both cases the pattern is the dual of my negative result. Distilling the search fails because no faithful forward trace exists (verdict-as-token); the escape is to compute the search offline into finite catalog, store it in the weights as memorized lookup, and leave only recall plus bounded verification in the trace. What transfers is therefore not search but memorization and verification. This sharpens the boundary beyond solvable vs. learnable: verifiable search is learnable as chain-of-thought exactly when its candidate structure compresses to catalog small enough to memorize and residual cheap 17 enough to verify in-budget. (The winners solver-coverage figures include train-set leakage by their own account, but their 91.57% held-out validation, on the same traintest generators use, confirms generalization within the distribution rather than row memorization.)

sections.title: 10 Limitations
sections.content: My study is deep on one benchmark rather than broad. did run controlled architecture sweep  (Table 6)  : training the identical cryptarithm corpus on dense Transformers (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3.54B) and an MoE Transformer (gpt-oss-20b) yields the same 0.04 floor as the hybrid, so the search-distillation ceiling is architecture-general rather than Mamba-specific. On scale, frontier models up to 671B sampled in-context under the competition budget also cap (0.05 / 0.00, Table 6). The one cell could not run is frontier model fine-tuned at length (and the exact 30B/A3B Transformer match, unschedulable on my compute). also report single training seeds per cell. The binomial CIs bound sampling noise but not seed variance. evaluate under greedy decoding because the grader does; sampling-based test-time search would change the achievable numbers (my hit@8 and pass@32 results quantify by how much) but not the single-trace learnability question study. Some internal figures deliberately did not promote into the headline claims because they appear in my run ledger but not in re-runnable report (e.g. pass@32 value attached to specific value-rule count); cite only the report-backed numbers and flag this in my released notes. Finally, did not win the competition: my best public score was 0.852 and my best private score 0.860, medal-class, but on submission public-LB selection would not have chosen (Section 8); the leader reached 0.92. regard that as orthogonal to the contribution, the value here is the instrumented account of the frontier, not the rank. And the cryptarithm ceiling does yield to an idea did not pursue: the memorizationcomputation split of the 1st-place solution (Section 9). My contribution is the mechanistic account of why distilling the search fails, which is precisely why that split is necessary.

sections.title: 11 Conclusion
sections.content: I set out to convert perfect symbolic solvers into small models chain-of-thought, on benchmark engineered so that held-out training accuracy is the test score. Most tasks transferred. One did not, and its resistance was not matter of effort: eleven CoT designs, RLVR, and STaR all left cryptarithm at 0.05, and five independent measurements agreed on why. The unifying lesson is that verifiable search is not learnable chain-of-thought. What small autoregressive model can imitate is forward computation; what search procedure requires is carried state, record of what has been tried and decision about when to stop, and when tasks only honest solution is search, SFT can learn only the shape of the solvers verdicts, which then misfire at inference. There are two ways across this gap, and both avoid distilling the search itself: teach the model search it can actually run (bit-manipulation, via STaR on its own successful traces), or remove the search from the trace entirely, precomputing its finite structure into catalog the model memorizes and verifies against (Section 9; the 1st-place solution). The right way to read solver coverage, then, is as necessary but badly insufficient condition: it tells you the answer exists, not that your model can reason its way to it, and least of all that the solvers search will become its chain-of-thought.

sections.title: Ethics and Broader Impact
sections.content: This work is diagnostic and defensive: it introduces no new model capability and no attack on deployed system. It does surface competition-integrity concerns the community should weigh: 18 public/private leaderboard split makes the public score lossy selector (Section 8), and shared high-scoring adapter can be trivially perturbed (adapter-noise laundering) into many ostensibly independent submissions. report these to inform benchmark and competition design (private-split selection, submission provenance), not to enable gaming, did not submit the noised adapter as my own work. Finally, the deterministic-generator benchmark is controlled proxy for procedural reasoning; my findings should not be read as claim about models general capability.

sections.title: Reproducibility and Artifacts
sections.content: Code, paper source, the architecture-control scripts, and per-row eval data are released at ht tps://github.com/harshpatel1692/search-not-learnable (interactive walkthrough at https://nemotron.harshpatel.live). release, per category: the reverse-engineered solvers and the synthetic-CoT renderers; the held-out evaluation harness; the per-row eval CSVs behind the figures; and the per-line fidelity audit (7,566 records). The benchmark data itself belongs to the competition and is available from Kaggle, not redistributed here. Numbers in this paper are drawn from dated experiment log. Where figure exists only in my run ledger and not in re-runnable report, say so rather than promote it.

sections.title: A Solver Pseudocode
sections.content: Each solver is the data-generation oracle for its category: it recovers the hidden rule from the worked examples and applies it to the query. Coverage is the fraction of the 9,500 training rows the solver answers correctly. give the four lookup/fit solvers compactly, then the two search solvers (equation and cryptarithm) in full; the bit-manipulation solver is in Section B. The four forward solvers (numeral, unit, gravity, cipher). numeral (100%) is fixed greedy subtractive encoder over the 13 Roman value-symbol pairs, it ignores the examples entirely. unit_conversion (100%) fits the linear map = ax + from the two most-separated example points (for numerical stability under the graders 102 tolerance) and applies it. gravity (100%) fits the . Equation_numeric (deduce 95.1%, guess 20.6%). Each example is AB CD = rhs with visible digits. The row has one global string mode (identity or reverse) and each operator glyph carries one operation from 13-op library (Algorithm 2). The policy locks the query operator to the first op in frequency-ordered list consistent with all of that glyphs examples, then accepts the mode only if every other glyph is also explainable under it. For an unseen query glyph (guess) the operation is undeducible, so the policy pins the mode from the known glyphs and bets the marginal-prior operation (sub), which is exactly the 20.6% marginal ceiling. Algorithm 2 Equation_numeric, policy_seq (deduce 95.1%, guess 20.6%) Require: examples grouped by glyph g; query (qa, qg, qb); op library (13 ops). Frequency order seq; default sign-render table 1: for mode (rev, id) do 2: qop first seq consistent with all examples of qg under mode if qg unseen then qop sub guess: marginal-prior bet if every other glyph also has some consistent op under mode then global-mode check return render(qa qop qb, mode, signpos(mode, qop)) 3: 4: 5: reverse first: generator prior 476:239 Cryptarithm (deduce: policy 67.7%, posterior vote 70.9%). This is the search solver, and the contrast between it and what forward CoT can imitate is the heart of the paper. The hidden per-row digitsymbol cipher is information-free, so no column reveals digit. The map is pinned only by backtracking search that requires all example equations to hold simultaneously, under the right per-glyph operation (from 30-op vocabulary), reading direction, and sign convention (Algorithm 3). Two sound prunes (units-digit congruence and full-operand forcing with injectivity) and most-constrained variable order keep the 10! assignment space tractable; the solver enumerates up to 3000 consistent assignments and scores each under two-regime (canonical + scrambled) mixture prior fit by EM, then returns the answer with maximum posterior mass. Crucially, the forward-only variant of this procedure (constraint propagation with no backtracking, Algorithm 4) is what left-to-right CoT could honestly reproduce, and it covers 1/659 of instances, which is why every supervised attempt to teach the search collapses (Section 5.2). Bit-Manipulation Solver, Basis, and Renderer Global single-rule search (98.88%). The production solver (Algorithm 5) treats the rule as one boolean function over up to three taps and searches composed expression grammar rather than the named basis directly. tap is transformed copy of the 8-bit input, rot/shl/shr by {0, . . . , 7}, shifts zero-filling at the boundary, giving 22 taps; not is folded into the grammar. The grammar enumerates boolean expressions over three projection variables by op-composition to depth 3 (ten two-input gates plus unary not), deduped by 8-bit truth table. For each expression the solver tries every assignment of distinct taps to its variables (a single var: 22; two: 22 21; three: perm(22, 3) = 9240), fail-fast on output bit 0 before checking bits 17, and accepts the first assignment that reproduces all eight bits of every example; it then applies that rule to the query. On the cache of all 1602 rows the solver finds consistent rule for every row but only 1584 (98.88%) generalize to the query, the 18-row gap is genuine over-fitting (a rule consistent on the shown examples that predicts the wrong query output). The same single-rule space, re-described in human terms, is the basis of Table 2; coverage by the basis ( 98% fit on examples) and 98.88% (correct on the held-out query) are different measurements and should not be conflated. 20 Algorithm 3 Cryptarithm, search solver cryptarithm2 (deduce vote 70.9%) Require: example equations {(Li, Ri)}, each LHS = s0s1 op s2s3. Query LHS; op vocab (30);

sections.content: 1: mass {} 2: for (rev, radix) {(, 10), (, 10), (, 11), (, 11)} do 3: radix 11 only as fallback normalize rows for rev (reverse magnitudes/result. Detect sign glyph); reject if sign glyph sits inside magnitude per glyph, restrict candidate ops by sign pattern and operand magnitude bounds. If all rows match structural concat, fix concat DFS over digitsymbol assignments (most-shared symbol first), pruned by units-digit congruence and full-operand forcing + injectivity, up to 3000 solutions for each solution σ do score logsumexp of canonical-regime and scrambled-regime log-priors of σs op assignment render the query answer under σ; mass[answer] += escore 4: 5: 6: 7: posterior-mass vote. Ties by max score then lexicographic 8: 9: return arg maxa mass[a] Algorithm 4 Cryptarithm-forward, the imitable forward closure ( 1/659 coverage) Require: committed (op, rev) per glyph; example equations 1: initialize every symbols digit domain to {0, . . . , 9} 2: repeat 3: units mod 10: narrow domains via (x y) mod 10 on placed units symbols magnitude bracket: narrow via result length / value range all-different cascade: remove singletons from other domains per-equation GAC: keep only operand/result digits that appear in some consistent (a, b) 6: 7: until no change or stalled 8: if every query operand symbol has singleton domain then return forward-derived answer 9: else return stalled the common case: 658/659 no teleporting 4: 5: Algorithm 5 Bit-manipulation, global single-rule search (98.88%, 1584/1602) Require: example pairs {(ini, outi)} of 8-bit strings; query input 1: the 22 taps {(rot, 0)} {(op, k) : op {rot, shl, shr}, = 1..7} 2: boolean expressions over vars {A, B, C}, composed to depth 3 (10 binary gates + not), deduped by truth table 3: for each expression in increasing depth do 4: for each assignment of distinct taps in to the variables used by do 5: 6: if reproduces output bit 0 of all examples then if reproduces all 8 bits of all examples then fail-fast 7: 8: return none return apply (with this tap assignment) to 1%: under-constrained / >3-tap tail; 5 time limit 21 Tap-count distribution (from the solve cache). The 1602 recovered rules split 0-tap 5, 1-tap 149, 2-tap 899 (56%), 3-tap 549 (34%). The twoand three-tap shares are exactly the strata in the models accuracy breakdown  (Table 4)  . The three-tap stratum is both the largest hard slice and the residual ceiling. The 18 over-fit failures (a rule consistent on the examples but wrong on the query) span 4 one-tap, 7 two-tap, and 7 three-tap rows. Verifiable rule inventory. Table 9 is the op-composition taxonomy of all 1602 recovered rules, read directly off the solve cache, the machine-checkable complement to the human-named basis of Table 2. The two views agree on the shape (xor dominant, large xnor stratum corresponding to the basiss or_xnor/choice families, identity/copy rules, and AND/OR combinations); the named basis is the interpretable face, this table is the reproducible ground truth. Table 9: The op-composition rule inventory over all 1602 training rows, computed directly from the solve cache (root operator of the recovered expression; multi-input rules compose these). Taps are near-uniform over rotate/shift by {1, . . . , 7}. Root operator Rows Tap count Rows xor or xnor and identity / copy not_a_and_b not_a_or_b constant nor 444 350 288 187 149 96 79 5 4 0 taps 1 tap 2 taps 3 taps tap type shl shr rot 5 149 899 549 1253 1197 1144 Per-bit stride renderer (84.6%, used only to author CoT). To narrate search the model could imitate, second solver expresses each output bit as constant, (possibly negated) copy of one input bit, or two-input gate of two input bits, with the referenced input positions advancing by fixed stride as increases (because taps are rotations/shifts). It greedily set-covers the eight output bits by maximal cyclic runs, preferring longer runs and simpler descriptors. Output bits needing three inputs are left uncovered and default to 1, the source of its 84.6% ceiling. This renderer is CoT authoring tool, not the production solver. As Section 5.1 reports, hand-authored narrations of either solver fail to transfer, and only STaR on the models own searches does.

sections.title: C Training Configuration and Submission History
sections.content: I used two training stacks. The early lean lineage (HuggingFace Trainer + PEFT) adapted only the projection modules. The later merge lineage (Unsloth + manual loop), which produced the banked 0.85 adapter, additionally adapts the attention projections and the unembedding  (Table 10)  . The trainable parameter count for the projection-only set is 880M (2.71%); the merge set is larger. Compute and the experiment ladder. Final adapters train on single RTX PRO 6000 (Blackwell, 9496 GB). The first end-to-end run measured 35 s/step. The Blackwell GPU required ptxas shim (locating the vendor binary, exporting TRITON_PTXAS_*, and pinning reported version) plus offline Mamba/causal-conv1d kernels. followed three-tier cheapest-first ladder: (i) 22 Table 10: LoRA SFT configuration for the two training stacks. The banked 0.85 adapter (v17) uses the merge lineage. Cheap iteration used managed LoRA service (Tinker) with the same rank and learning rate. Its checkpoints require an SVD rank-32 conversion before submission. Setting Lean lineage (v9v14) Merge lineage (v15v19; banked v17) LoRA rank / α / dropout target modules learning rate schedule optimizer effective batch epochs max sequence length precision 32 / 32 / 0.0 32 / 16 / 0.05 in/out/up/down proj (4) + q/k/v/o proj, lm_head (9) 2 104 cosine, warmup 0.03 AdamW 16 (2 8) step-capped , bf16 2 104 linear decay AdamW β(0.9, 0.95), ε 108, wd 0 32 (micro 4 8) 2 (category-stratified) 8192 LoRA fp32 / router fp32 / base bf16 zero-cost base-model API probes on the 30B target and 120B reference; (ii) Tinker LoRA rounds (rank 32, LR 2 104 fresh / 5 106 resume, batch 32, prompt tokens loss-masked) at few dollars per round and cents per held-out eval, under per-run dollar caps (GRPO runs capped at $1); (iii) the Kaggle from-base final, which avoids the SVD conversion tax that Tinker checkpoint incurs ( 0.025 LB on my calibration). Table 11: Submission history. LB is the hidden-test score. The banked result is v17; v18 adds bit STaR within noise; v19 was parked. The null adapter (all = 0) reproduces the base and certifies the pipeline. Version LB Change Note first end-to-end; easy-4 easy-4, improved cipher CoT 0.52 v9 / v10 0.57 v12 0.59 + early bit CoT v13 0.54 v14 0.50 null adapter TinkerSVD 0.79 0.82 v15 0.72 v16 0.85 v17 0.84 + bit STaR (additive) v18 + eq/crypt bets v19 per-bit tap CoT (regression) 0-step (B=0) v16 corpus, converted new grammars, warm-start compressed bit dialect from-base merge, 2e4 2 banked , lean base 0.466 SVD tax anchoring failure regression within noise parked (no LB) Loss convergence vs. learning. Every SFT run drove training loss to the floor quickly and regardless of whether the procedure was learnable: the Kaggle merge runs log final loss 0.003, the Tinker warm-start reaches NLL 0.001, and the cryptarithm rounds reach NLL 0.146 by step 35 and the floor by their second epoch. Yet held-out cryptarithm accuracy stayed in [0.01, 0.07] across all fourteen rounds  (Table 12)  . Train loss is not signal for this kind of learnability; only held-out verification is. (Full per-step loss curves were not persisted; recovered checkpoint NLLs and the final-loss tail, so report convergence points rather than curves.) 23 Table 12: Full cheap-iteration run ledger (Tinker), the training log behind the experiment ladder. Held-out acc is the runs target-category accuracy recovered from the eval oracle (cryptarithm rounds report deduce accuracy. Bit rounds report overall bit accuracy); is the multi-category weighted oracle. $ is list-price estimate. The pattern is the paper in one table: cryptarithm rounds consume real compute and never move; bit climbs only under STaR.

sections.title: D Tokenizer Analysis
sections.content: The tokenizer is byte-level BPE: 131,072 vocabulary entries, 269,443 merges. unk_token is None and byte-fallback is off (so coverage is via the byte alphabet, and no <unk> is emitted in practice, though an <unk> symbol occupies id 0). There are 1,000 added tokens, including the reasoning delimiters <think>/</think> and 982 reserved <SPECIAL_n> slots. ASCII-only is real constraint. Unicode logic/math glyphs fragment into rare multi-byte pieces, which the model handles poorly even though no <unk> fires; ASCII spellings are one or two common tokens  (Table 13)  . therefore write all chain-of-thought in ASCII, operators as words (XOR/OR/AND/NOT), -> for arrows, != for inequality, ok/no for /=. Digits tokenize one-per-digit, so an 8-bit string is exactly 8 tokens, convenient for the bit task. 24 Table 13: Token counts for Unicode operators vs. their ASCII spellings under the competition tokenizer (measured). The fragments are rare byte-pieces. Unicode tokens ASCII tokens (XOR) = / (rare) 3 3 2 2 2 1 XOR OR/AND/NOT -> != ok/no XNOR 2 1 1 1 1 3 Lengths (measured on the actual data). Prompts are short and CoT terser still  (Table 14)  , both far under the 7680-token budget. The gap between the 274-token synthetic CoT train on and the 1k3.8k tokens the model generates at inference  (Table 17)  is the bases native verbosity reasserting itself, and is part of why authored brevity does not survive to inference. Table 14: Prompt and synthetic-CoT token lengths by category (measured with the competition tokenizer over all training rows). Cryptarithm CoT was rendered per round (Section 5.2), not in this synthetic corpus. Category prompt (med / max) synth CoT (med / max) numeral unit_conversion gravity cipher bit_manipulation equation_numeric cryptarithm 61 / 72 87 / 102 128 / 152 118 / 170 240 / 259 7580 / 96 6264 / 99 / 154 181 / 184 150 / 157 168 / 222 221 / 274 188 / 213 , (per round) Budget. The 7680-token budget (with max_model_len = 8192) is comfortable for most categories. Free-run generation medians are 1k (numeral) to 3.8k (bit). It binds only on rendered cryptarithm search traces, which loop to the cap, one reason STaRs shorter self-traces, which cut bit truncation from 18.6% to 0.2%, helped. Per-token difficulty audit. Logging per-token cross-entropy over the training CoT (6,755 token types) gives vocabulary-discipline tool  (Table 15)  . Two things stand out. First, the count-weighted mean CE ranks the categories: the lookup/fit tasks sit at 2.13.2 while cryptarithm and cipher sit at 5.96.3, the harder categories are written in tokens the base finds more surprising (rare themed cipher words, cryptarithm glyphs, enumeration). Second, even the boolean operator words adopted to stay ASCII carry real surprise (-> CE 6.9 over 333k uses, OR 6.5, NOT 7.3, AND 8.7, majority 10.4), reinforcing the native-voice finding: the CoT vocabulary is itself somewhat out-of-distribution for the base. The audit flags 1,253-token blacklist (CE 7, seen 100; 771 of them cipher vocabulary) that linter then keeps out of authored traces. (High token CE is rarity, not an accuracy, signal: cipher has the highest token CE yet 0.99 accuracy, because its rare tokens are answer-bearing vocabulary words.) 25 Table 15: Count-weighted mean per-token cross-entropy of the training CoT, by category (token audit. Lower = the base finds the trace tokens less surprising). The lookup/fit tasks are cheapest. Cryptarithm and cipher are written in the most surprising tokens. Category mean CE Category mean CE unit_conversion gravity numeral bit_manipulation 2.09 2.27 3.21 4.15 equation_num. deduce equation_num. guess cryptarithm deduce cryptarithm guess cipher 4.97 5.19 5.85 5.98 6. Table 17 gives the length and failure-type breakdown that grounds Section 6: for the shipped adapter, failures are wrong-answer rather than truncation in every category except the cryptarithm search renders.
